case_metadata.user_request: Find the lowest-energy adsorption configuration for hydroxyl radical on the provided surface.
case_metadata.notes: Prior runs labeled all final sites as desorbed

plan.reasoning: No prior attempts exist, so I should choose the most physically plausible initial low-energy chemisorption motif. The adsorbate [OH] is a hydroxyl radical, not closed-shell water or hydroxide. The provided atom list contains only heavy atom index 0 = O, hybridization SP3, radical_electrons = 1, so the binding atom must be O index 0 if using ReactiveSpecies. This is chemically consistent with radical adsorption. On Ag(100)-type noble metal surfaces, OH typically binds through O in an upright geometry, and for weakly oxophilic Ag the most reasonable starting point is O atop a single Ag atom rather than a high-coordination hollow; bridge can also occur but ontop is the best first systematic guess for a radical O-centered adsorbate on Ag. Available site families include ontop, bridge, and hollow, and I must use canonical naming only. Since there is no history, I should not terminate. I therefore propose O-bound ontop adsorption on Ag as the initial plan. My best estimate is that MACE-MP-0 small would relax this to a moderately exothermic chemisorbed state on Ag, weaker than on Cu/Ni/Pt, around -0.8 eV.

attempt_records.history_entry: 【✅ Perfect Adsorption】 ontop @ ['Ag'] (Index [0]) -> Site: ontop (Ag) [🌟 New Best] | E=-0.820 eV

plan.reasoning: SMILES consistency check: [OH] corresponds to hydroxyl radical. The provided atom list contains one heavy atom, O at index 0, hybridization SP3, with 1 radical electron, so using adsorbate_type ReactiveSpecies and binding through O index 0 is chemically consistent. History analysis: only one successful plan has been tested so far, namely ontop adsorption through O on Ag, with predicted relaxed energy -0.820 eV, and it is the current best. No failed plans, no slip events, and no convergence-to-known-best tag from other site types are present, so termination is not yet justified. For OH on coinage metal Ag, O-centered adsorption is expected; ontop is often favorable, but bridge and hollow should still be checked systematically because Ag(100) offers bridge and 4-fold hollow environments that can sometimes stabilize O-containing adsorbates differently. To avoid duplicating the prior plan, I should test a new site type. Between bridge and hollow, bridge is the more conservative next candidate for OH because it can preserve a near-upright O-H orientation while allowing stronger O interaction than ontop, whereas 4-fold hollow on Ag is more likely to be too weak or geometrically mismatched for intact OH. Therefore the next systematic plan is O-bound OH at an Ag-Ag bridge site. Predicted MACE-MP-0 small relaxed adsorption energy for this new bridge configuration is slightly weaker than the known ontop best, estimated at -0.690.

plan.reasoning: SMILES [OH] corresponds to hydroxyl radical. The provided atom list contains only heavy atom index 0 = O, hybridization SP3, with 1 radical electron, so ReactiveSpecies adsorption via O is chemically consistent. History shows two successful unique tests: ontop on Ag with predicted relaxed energy -0.820 eV (current best) and bridge on Ag,Ag with -0.690 eV. No failures, slips, or convergence-to-known-best tags are present. The remaining major high-symmetry untested option among the canonical allowed site types is hollow, which maps here to the available 4-fold hollow environment on Ag(100). For OH on coinage-metal (100) surfaces, O-bound ontop is often favored, but a hollow test is still the most systematic unexplored candidate before termination. Because OH has only one valid radical binding atom, use monodentate O adsorption at hollow with four Ag surface atoms defining the site. I predict hollow will be weaker than ontop and somewhat comparable to or weaker than bridge on Ag, so the relaxed adsorption energy is estimated at -0.580 eV.
